market_summary.narrative: Markets staged a powerful 3-day rally from April 7-9 driven by the surprise U.S.-Iran two-week ceasefire agreement, sending the S&P 500 up +3.57% for the week to close at 6,817.89—its best week of 2026. The Nasdaq Composite surged +4.73% to 22,907.95 and the Russell 2000 gained +3.96% to 2,630.64, with Industrials leading all sectors (+5.6%) as oil volatility eased and growth expectations firmed. The ceasefire triggered WTI crude's largest single-day decline since 2020 (falling 16% on April 7), while the March CPI print (+3.3% YoY headline, +2.6% core—below the feared +3.4% consensus) delivered a second catalyst, confirming the macro regime shift from Slowdown through Transition to Reflation in just three days. Friday's slight S&P 500 pull-back (-0.11%) reflected lingering Strait of Hormuz uncertainty and renewed AI software sector pressure from enterprise SaaS downgrades.
market_summary.regime_description: Growth accelerating with rising inflation. Favors cyclicals, value, energy, financials.

value_picks.name: Capital One Financial Corporation
value_picks.ev_ebitda: N/A (financial company; normalized PE ~9-10x ex-merger costs)
value_picks.revenue_growth: +36.6% YoY (total revenues before provisions: $53.4B FY2025 vs $39.1B FY2024; Discover acquisition added scale)
value_picks.why_undervalued: COF's trailing P/E of 57x appears inflated solely due to ~$5B in one-time Discover Financial acquisition and integration charges booked in FY2025; on a normalized basis, the combined entity trades at approximately 9-10x earnings. The stock is 25.7% below its 52-week high despite the company generating $26.1B in free cash flow in FY2025 and $27.7B in operating cash flow. Markets are applying a merger-uncertainty discount that is rapidly dissipating as integration milestones are met.
value_picks.bull_case: The Discover acquisition creates the largest U.S. credit card network by receivables, with management guiding to $1.5B+ in annual synergies from network cost savings and cross-sell opportunities. As one-time charges roll off and synergy benefits materialize through 2026-2027, reported EPS will dramatically re-rate upward; analysts at JPMorgan (upgraded Overweight April 9, target $213), Wells Fargo, and Goldman Sachs all have $260+ targets, implying 37% upside from current levels. The 100% bullish analyst consensus with zero neutral or bearish ratings is exceptionally rare for a $120B financial company.

value_picks.name: Synchrony Financial
value_picks.ev_ebitda: N/A (financial company; EV/EBITDA metric not comparable for consumer finance)
value_picks.revenue_growth: +1.4% YoY (net income FY2025 $3.55B vs FY2024 $3.50B; stabilizing credit losses in H2 2025)
value_picks.why_undervalued: SYF trades at 7.46x trailing earnings — roughly half the multiple of major bank peers — despite generating $9.85B in free cash flow and demonstrating credit loss normalization through H2 2025 after elevated charge-off rates in 2024 depressed sentiment. The stock sits 18.4% below its 52-week high with a forthcoming earnings catalyst on April 21, 2026 that could unlock re-rating. At its current price, the implied FCF yield of 37.8% prices in a recessionary credit scenario that appears inconsistent with improving macroeconomic data.
value_picks.bull_case: The recently closed Ally Financial credit card partnership acquisition adds a substantial new receivables base and diversifies SYF's partnerships beyond legacy retail. As consumer credit normalization continues into 2026 — with charge-off rates declining sequentially in each of the last three quarters — the overhang that compressed the multiple should lift. Street consensus shows zero bearish analysts with a 17.2% implied upside to the $84.87 average target; a reversion to even 10x earnings implies a $100+ share price.

value_picks.name: United Airlines Holdings, Inc.
value_picks.why_undervalued: UAL trades at 9.45x trailing earnings and 6.52x EV/EBITDA, a dramatic discount to legacy airline peer multiples of 15-20x P/E and the broader S&P 500. The stock is 19.1% below its 52-week high despite consistently beating earnings estimates and generating $2.6B in free cash flow in FY2025. Macro uncertainty around jet fuel costs from the Iran conflict has created an overhang, yet UAL's hedging program and revenue yield improvements have more than offset fuel cost pressures in recent quarters.
value_picks.bull_case: Every single sell-side analyst covering UAL rates it a buy (14/0/0), a distinction shared by almost no S&P 500 large-cap, with a consensus target of $134.14 implying 39.1% upside from current levels. United's premium cabin expansion (United Polaris and Premium Plus), international route ramp, and loyalty program monetization are structural earnings drivers that justify a higher multiple. In a reflation environment with rising consumer spending on experiences and travel, UAL's revenue per available seat mile continues to expand; management's own 2026 guidance targets $4B+ in adjusted pre-tax income.

value_picks.name: Devon Energy Corporation
value_picks.why_undervalued: DVN trades at only 4.21x EV/EBITDA — a deep discount to E&P peers averaging 6-7x — despite strong Permian and Delaware Basin production growth and a $2.8B FCF profile. The pending all-stock merger with Coterra Energy announced in early 2026 creates uncertainty-driven selling pressure among index-rebalancing funds, compressing the stock even as the combined entity would become one of the largest diversified U.S. energy producers. Activist Kimmeridge's stake underscores the view that management must either deliver superior returns or execute the merger accretively. Note: at -9.3% below the 52-week high, DVN does not fully meet the 15-20% threshold but is included due to its superior energy-sector setup and pending re-rating event.
value_picks.bull_case: The Coterra-Devon merger, if completed, creates a ~$50B+ enterprise value combined E&P with diversified multi-basin assets (Permian, Anadarko, Marcellus), superior cost structure, and a high variable dividend policy that should attract income-oriented investors. In a reflation macro regime with crude above $90, Devon's capital-efficient Permian drilling program generates material per-share FCF growth; Raymond James recently raised its target to $52. Strong Buy consensus (11/2/0) and the merger optionality provide a floor, while energy's tailwinds in 2026 amid ongoing Middle East supply disruptions support the commodity price deck.

value_picks.name: Occidental Petroleum Corporation
value_picks.revenue_growth: -0.5% YoY ($22.1B FY2025 vs $22.2B FY2024; stable production volumes offset by oil price mix)
value_picks.why_undervalued: OXY trades at 5.83x EV/EBITDA, a meaningful discount to integrated energy major peers, despite owning some of the highest-quality Permian Basin acreage in the U.S. and generating $4.1B in free cash flow. The elevated trailing P/E of 43x reflects the CrownRock acquisition cost amortization and higher depreciation charges, not underlying earnings weakness. Berkshire Hathaway — which holds approximately 28% of OXY's shares outstanding — continues to add to its position, providing both a valuation floor and governance credibility that the market has not fully priced in.
value_picks.bull_case: Warren Buffett's active accumulation of OXY shares at prices between $55-65 per share constitutes the most visible 'smart money' endorsement in the energy sector; Berkshire's SEC filings confirm continued buying into April 2026. OXY's low-sulfur, light crude from the Permian commands premium pricing amid Middle East supply disruptions, and the company's carbon capture subsidiary (OxyChem's direct air capture facility in Texas) positions it favorably for energy transition capital flows. Debt reduction following the CrownRock deal completion reduces interest burden and improves per-share FCF meaningfully through 2026-2027, with Wells Fargo maintaining Overweight with a $72 target.

momentum_picks.name: GE Vernova Inc.
momentum_picks.catalyst: GE Vernova entered 2026 with a $150 billion equipment and services backlog, and in January raised its 2026 revenue guidance to $44–$45 billion (from $41–$42 billion) while lifting free cash flow guidance to $5–$5.5 billion and doubling its dividend. The Power segment is guiding for 16–18% organic revenue growth in 2026, with the Electrification segment targeting $13.5–$14 billion in revenue as direct data center orders tripled year-over-year in 2025 and Goldman Sachs raised its price target to $1,000 in April 2026. Upcoming Q1 2026 earnings (April 22) are expected to show 91% EPS growth year-over-year, providing a near-term catalyst for the stock.
momentum_picks.risk_reward: At $991, GEV trades at a significant premium to its 52-week low of $252 and approaching the $1,000 psychological level with Goldman Sachs' recently raised target, suggesting the near-term upside is increasingly priced in. The primary risk is the Wind segment, which is expected to generate ~$400 million in EBITDA losses in 2026, and any broader tariff escalation on imported components could compress margins on new orders. The risk/reward is skewed moderately positive for patient holders, as the $64 billion equipment backlog already carrying 6 incremental margin points versus current earnings is not fully reflected in consensus estimates, with the 2028 EBITDA margin target of 20% representing substantial upside to the current 9% level.

momentum_picks.name: Comfort Systems USA, Inc.
momentum_picks.catalyst: Comfort Systems reported a blowout Q4 2025, with EPS of $9.37 versus $6.75 expected (38% beat) and revenue up 41.7% year-over-year, while its construction backlog nearly doubled to $11.94 billion by year-end 2025 — more than its entire FY2025 revenue — driven by hyperscaler data center demand that grew from 33% to 45% of total revenue in a single year. The company is expanding modular manufacturing capacity from 3 to 4 million square feet in 2026 and guided for mid-to-high-teen same-store revenue growth, with consensus projecting $36.60 EPS in FY2026 (+26.7%). DA Davidson raised its price target to $1,800, and FIX hit a new 52-week high of $1,547 in early April as energy-infrastructure spending accelerated.
momentum_picks.risk_reward: FIX trades at approximately 43x forward earnings, well above its 5-year median of 22x, implying the market is pricing in sustained above-market execution — any miss on backlog conversion pace or gross margin (the critical 25%+ threshold breached in Q4 2025) would likely cause rapid multiple compression. Labor cost inflation and project delays remain the primary execution risks, particularly as the company scales rapidly across new geographies through recent acquisitions. On the positive side, $11.94 billion in legally committed, priced backlog essentially locks in FY2026 revenues without requiring new contract wins, and the analysts' mean target of $1,645 implies ~3% upside from the current price with DA Davidson's $1,800 bull case representing a more compelling 13% upside.

momentum_picks.name: Freeport-McMoRan Inc.
momentum_picks.catalyst: FCX surged 7% on April 8 as copper prices rallied sharply, with global copper near $6.00 per pound driven by AI data center electrification demand and supply deficits from the ongoing Grasberg mine disruption — the restart of Production Blocks 2 and 3 at the Grasberg mine in Q2 2026 represents a major near-term operational catalyst that will restore production volumes and free cash flow. Bank of America raised its price target to $81 from $68 in April 2026, while Freedom Capital upgraded to Buy with a $76 target, reflecting confidence that sustained copper demand from power grid buildouts and EV charging infrastructure will underpin elevated prices through the cycle. The company's proprietary leaching technology program in North America is expected to add incremental low-capex copper output, further supporting the earnings ramp into 2026.
momentum_picks.risk_reward: FCX's revenue growth was modest at +1.8% in FY2025 due to the Grasberg disruption, but consensus forecasts 27.7% adjusted EPS growth for FY2026 as volumes normalize — this operational recovery story offers compelling upside if Grasberg ramps on schedule in Q2. The key risks are a reversal in copper prices (historically volatile), geopolitical complications with Indonesia's 51% ownership stake in PT Freeport Indonesia, and any recurrence of operational incidents at Grasberg. Valuation is supportive relative to history, with FCX up 42% over the past six months yet still trading at 21x forward earnings against a consensus target of $81 (BofA), implying ~19% further upside from current levels.

momentum_picks.name: Nucor Corporation
momentum_picks.catalyst: Nucor raised its Consumer Spot Price to $1,025 per ton in March 2026, pushing hot-rolled coil prices above the $1,000 milestone for the first time in a structural shift driven by doubled import tariffs (Section 232 at 50%) and rising demand from AI data center and infrastructure construction — the company guided Q1 2026 EPS of $2.70–$2.80, representing a 264% increase year-over-year and a 60% sequential jump from Q4 2025. The steel mills segment backlog entered 2026 up 40% year-over-year, management confirmed shipment volumes will grow 5% in 2026 as infrastructure capex from public and private sectors accelerates, and Wells Fargo raised its price target to $194 citing the domestic market's insulation from foreign competition. Full Q1 2026 results are due April 27.
momentum_picks.risk_reward: Nucor offers the strongest fundamental support among the 5 picks, with FY2026 consensus EPS of $11.76 (+52.5% from FY2025's $7.71) and an average analyst price target of $190.85 implying ~3% upside from current levels — the stock's relatively modest 3-month return of +13.65% suggests momentum is still early versus peers, making it an attractive entry point. The primary risks are a reversal in steel pricing if import tariffs are rolled back or if construction demand slows faster than expected, and the company's exposure to a potential housing slowdown. Nucor's electric arc furnace model and diversified end markets (automotive, construction, manufacturing, energy) provide downside resilience that most steel peers lack.

momentum_picks.name: California Resources Corporation
momentum_picks.catalyst: California Resources is the only oil and gas producer in California, providing it a monopolistic market position in the state's energy market with virtually no in-state competition, while its Carbon TerraVault CCS (Carbon Capture and Storage) project is advancing toward first commercial injection in early 2026 — backed by seven Class VI permits under EPA review — creating a new high-margin revenue stream that diversifies the company beyond hydrocarbons. Elevated crude oil prices (driven by Middle East geopolitical risk and Iran-related supply concerns in 2026) have turbocharged FCFs, as evidenced by a 104.3% one-year total shareholder return; management plans to increase drilling rigs to four in early 2026 as permitting stabilizes, driving production growth at historically low break-even costs. Q1 2026 results are due May 5, with the market anticipating continued cash flow outperformance.
momentum_picks.risk_reward: The 1-week return turned slightly negative (-1.73%) as de-escalation signals from Iran eased the geopolitical risk premium embedded in oil prices, highlighting the stock's sensitivity to crude — a sustained decline below $80/bbl Brent would pressure free cash flow and likely reverse the YTD gains. CRC's California-only operations create regulatory concentration risk, as any adverse state energy policy shift or regulatory delay on CCS permitting could impair the carbon management business case. The bull case is compelling given the CCS optionality (currently unmonetized) and monopolistic oil production position, with analysts' consensus price target of $60.75 representing modest downside from current levels but the high end target of $68 consistent with current price, suggesting the market has largely priced in the near-term fundamental thesis.

trends.title: Iran Ceasefire Unlocks Oil and Risk-Appetite Reset
trends.description: President Trump's April 7 ceasefire agreement with Iran—contingent on Strait of Hormuz reopening—triggered WTI crude's largest single-day drop since 2020, falling 16.4% from ~$112 to ~$94 before recovering to ~$96 by week-end as Iran accused the U.S. of breaching ceasefire terms. The oil volatility collapse unlocked a massive risk-on rotation: Industrials surged 5.6%, financials and tech rallied hard, while Energy was the sole losing sector (-3.77%). With Polymarket giving only ~22.5% odds of a durable ceasefire by April 30 and ~51.5% by June 30, the situation remains fragile but the directional signal was enough to drive the regime shift.

trends.title: March CPI Surprise Softens Fed Tightening Fears—Briefly
trends.description: The March CPI print came in at +3.3% YoY headline (vs. +3.4% consensus) and +2.6% core (vs. +2.7% consensus), meaningfully below the feared acceleration. The softer-than-expected result reduced near-term rate hike fears and supported the ceasefire rally, pushing the 10-year Treasury yield down to 4.31% from prior highs. However, the Fed's dot plot still shows only one cut in 2026, and seven of 19 members projected no cuts at all—with the FOMC noting it needs to remain 'nimble' given war-related inflationary tail risks. The April 28-29 FOMC meeting becomes the next critical data point.

trends.title: Bank Earnings Season Opens Monday with High Expectations
trends.description: Q1 2026 bank earnings begin April 13 (GS), with JPM, C, WFC on April 14 and MS, BAC on April 15. Consensus expects Goldman at $16.48 EPS (+16.7% YoY, revenue +12.9% to $17.0B), JPMorgan at $5.49 EPS (+8% YoY), and Bank of America at $1.01 EPS (+12% YoY). All six banks rallied into earnings this week—JPM +5.1%, GS +5.2%, MS +7.1%, BAC +6.5%, WFC +6.0%—with the street expecting strong trading revenues from the Iran volatility and elevated M&A pipelines. The key risk is whether management teams guide cautiously given geopolitical uncertainty and credit reserve build expectations.

trends.title: AI Semiconductor Boom Accelerates; Storage Stocks Surge
trends.description: The AI semiconductor theme dominated the week's gainers, with INTC (+23.9%), SNDK (+21.3%), MRVL (+19.9%), TER (+18.9%), AVGO (+18.2%), STX (+17.7%), and WDC (+16.5%) all surging. Bank of America raised its 2026 global semiconductor forecast to $1.3 trillion (+$300B revision) and identified NVDA, AVGO, MRVL, and AMD as top drivers. Intel's 18A node entering high-volume manufacturing and NVIDIA's $5B strategic equity stake validated the domestic foundry thesis. Hyperscaler AI capex commitments for 2026 now exceed $650B across MSFT, AMZN, GOOG, and META.

trends.title: Enterprise SaaS Under Siege from AI 'Seat Compression' Narrative
trends.description: A UBS downgrade of ServiceNow on April 10 on 'seat compression' fears—AI automation reducing human users of per-seat licensed software—triggered one of the sharpest SaaS sector selloffs of 2026. NOW fell -18.6%, HUBS -21.4%, SNOW -20.3%, NET -21.0%, GWRE -21.8%, OKTA -21.5%, INTU -16.9%, and WDAY -14.9% for the week. Michael Burry's (since-deleted) social media post about Anthropic competition amplified PLTR's -13.7% drop. The bifurcation between AI infrastructure winners and incumbent SaaS losers is the defining intra-market dynamic of the Reflation regime.

risks.title: Ceasefire Fragility and Strait of Hormuz Reopening Uncertainty
risks.description: Iran publicly accused the U.S. of ceasefire breaches by Thursday (April 9), and Strait of Hormuz maritime traffic remains restricted despite the ceasefire. Prediction markets assign only 22.5% probability to a durable deal by April 30. Any breakdown in negotiations—or reports of 'ghost mines' and continued blockade activity—could reverse the oil rally and trigger an abrupt risk-off episode. WTI is still $29/barrel above pre-conflict levels ($67), meaning the war risk premium has not been fully unwound.

risks.title: Persistent Inflation Could Force Fed to Flip Hawkish
risks.description: Despite the softer March CPI, inflation at +3.3% YoY remains 165bps above the Fed's 2% target, and the Fed's own revised projections now show PCE inflation ending 2026 at 2.7%. Seven of 19 FOMC members project zero cuts in 2026, and CME data shows 70% probability rates hold at 3.50-3.75% through January 2027. If April/May CPI data re-accelerate—particularly if the Strait of Hormuz remains partially restricted—markets could rapidly price out the remaining single cut, compressing equity multiples that assume gradual easing.

risks.title: Weak U.S. Growth Signal (GDPNow 1.3%) Contradicts Reflation Thesis
risks.description: The Atlanta Fed GDPNow estimate as of week-end stands at approximately 1.3% for Q1 2026, well below the Reflation regime's implied growth expansion. If Q1 GDP data (due late April) confirms a significant growth slowdown while inflation remains elevated, the market could face a stagflationary re-pricing—particularly dangerous given the S&P 500 Shiller CAPE of ~38.9x (historically associated with severe drawdowns when inflation rises). This risk was flagged Friday by late-session profit-taking.

risks.title: AI 'Seat Compression' Threatens High-Multiple SaaS Valuations
risks.description: The UBS ServiceNow downgrade catalyzed a violent sector rotation that wiped 15-22% off leading enterprise SaaS names in a single week. If the Anthropic 'Claude Mythos' model and similar AI agents genuinely compress software seat counts, the $2T+ SaaS industry faces structural multiple compression. Many watchlist names—NOW, HUBS, SNOW, NET, OKTA, INTU—remain at elevated forward multiples despite the selloff, and Q1 earnings commentary from these companies (due May) will be critical for validating or invalidating the thesis.

risks.title: Bank Earnings Guidance Risk and Credit Reserve Build
risks.description: While consensus bank EPS estimates for Q1 are strong (+8-17% YoY), the key risk is management guidance. JPMorgan's Jamie Dimon has consistently flagged geopolitical and credit risks, and JPM's $103B NII guidance for full-year 2026 implies limited upside. War-related loan reserve builds, energy sector credit exposure, and potential softening in consumer credit quality (post-inflation squeeze) could drive downside guidance surprises. Any negative guidance from GS or JPM on April 13-14 could reverse the financial sector's +5-7% weekly gains.

watchlist_movers.update: Fastly crashed -31.2% on massive insider selling ($40M+ sold in past 90 days by CEO/CTO), weak consensus outlook (Hold, $13 avg target vs ~$30 stock), and broader CDN/edge sector de-rating from AI competition concerns.

Intel surged on commencement of high-volume 18A node shipments and finalization of $5B strategic investment from NVIDIA, validating its IDM 2.0 turnaround. Stock roughly doubled over the past 30 days.
Akamai fell -22.5% as stock dropped 12.4% on Friday alone and 7.1% Thursday; soft FY2026 guidance (EPS miss vs consensus) and concerns about AI edge competition weighed heavily on the CDN incumbent.

Guidewire Software fell -21.8% on UBS downgrade of ServiceNow that triggered sector-wide enterprise software selloff; "seat compression" narrative—AI reducing per-seat software users—hit high-multiple SaaS names hard.

Okta fell -21.5% on AI cybersecurity fears (Anthropic "Claude Mythos" model concerns) and broader enterprise software de-rating; down -22% over week despite no company-specific fundamental deterioration.
HubSpot crashed -21.4%; down -50.3% YTD. UBS's ServiceNow downgrade on April 10 triggered broad CRM/marketing software rout on AI seat compression fears. Trading 71.7% below 52-week high of $672.24.
SanDisk (recently spun off from Western Digital) surged on AI storage demand wave; storage sector re-rating as hyperscaler HDD demand tightens dramatically.
Cloudflare fell -21.0% on AI competition fears in CDN/security space; Anthropic "Project Glasswing" cyber AI narrative hurt sector broadly alongside AKAM and FSLY.
WIX fell 20.5% on the week, likely driven by enterprise SaaS/software de-rating on AI seat compression fears and Anthropic threat narrative.
Snowflake fell -20.3% on broader SaaS de-rating; AI platform competition concerns and "seat compression" narrative hit data cloud stocks alongside the enterprise software sector rout.
Marvell surged on BofA semiconductor upgrade raising 2026 chip forecast to $1.3T and naming MRVL as top AI data-center custom silicon driver. Up ~20% on the week.
BRZE fell 19.9% on the week, likely driven by enterprise SaaS/software de-rating on AI seat compression fears and Anthropic threat narrative.
IOT fell 19.9% on the week, likely driven by enterprise SaaS/software de-rating on AI seat compression fears and Anthropic threat narrative.
Teradyne surged as semiconductor test equipment demand surged alongside chip sector re-rating; beneficiary of Intel 18A ramp and broader AI chip capex cycle.
PCOR fell 18.7% on the week, likely driven by enterprise SaaS/software de-rating on AI seat compression fears and Anthropic threat narrative.

ServiceNow fell -18.6%; UBS downgraded NOW on April 10 citing "seat compression" from AI automation, triggering sector-wide enterprise software selloff. This was the catalyst for the week's software rout.
Broadcom rallied strongly on AI chip tailwinds; BofA named AVGO as a top driver in upgraded $1.3T semiconductor forecast. Stock up ~18% on the week.
AMPL fell 17.9% on the week, likely driven by enterprise SaaS/software de-rating on AI seat compression fears and Anthropic threat narrative.
Seagate hit new 52-week high after analyst named it top IT hardware pick; non-GAAP operating income +67.5% YoY; stock up 17.7% on week, +582% over 52 weeks on AI nearline HDD demand.
Intuit fell -16.9% on enterprise SaaS de-rating following NOW downgrade; concerns about AI disruption to SMB software incumbents. Stock down -17% week.
PATH fell 16.7% on the week, likely driven by enterprise SaaS/software de-rating on AI seat compression fears and Anthropic threat narrative.
BL fell 16.6% on the week, likely driven by enterprise SaaS/software de-rating on AI seat compression fears and Anthropic threat narrative.
Western Digital surged on AI storage demand; firm purchase orders through calendar 2026 from top hyperscalers. Stock up +16.5% on week, +540% over prior 52 weeks. Analysts see further upside.
Arista Networks surged +16.3% on ceasefire-driven tech rally and continued AI networking demand; stock benefited from hyperscaler capex cycle and strong Q1 order momentum.
NTNX fell 16.3% on the week, likely driven by enterprise SaaS/software de-rating on AI seat compression fears and Anthropic threat narrative.

DOCN fell 16.0% on the week, likely driven by enterprise SaaS/software de-rating on AI seat compression fears and Anthropic threat narrative.
Corning surged +15.8% on the week; up ~57% YTD driven by $6B Meta fiber optic supply deal for AI data centers and plant expansion announcement in North Carolina.
WDAY fell 14.9% on the week, likely driven by enterprise SaaS/software de-rating on AI seat compression fears and Anthropic threat narrative.
ZS fell 14.9% on the week, likely driven by enterprise SaaS/software de-rating on AI seat compression fears and Anthropic threat narrative.
Micron surged +14.9% on AI storage demand wave alongside Seagate and Western Digital; BofA chip upgrade and tightening HBM/DRAM supply drove sector re-rating.

Palantir fell -13.7% despite Trump Truth Social endorsement ("great war fighting capabilities"); Michael Burry deleted post claiming Anthropic competition, broad AI multiple compression, and -30% YTD context drove selling pressure.
Amazon surged +13.7% on broad tech rally post-ceasefire; beneficiary of hyperscaler AI capex cycle with ~$100B+ planned capex for 2026; consumer unit also buoyed by ceasefire-driven macro optimism.
VEEV fell 13.0% on the week, likely driven by enterprise SaaS/software de-rating on AI seat compression fears and Anthropic threat narrative.

AMD surged +12.6% on BofA chip sector upgrade and AI GPU/data center momentum; named alongside NVDA and AVGO as key AI beneficiary in revised $1.3T semiconductor forecast.
